name: Aisha bint Abu Bakr
title: Aisha bint Abu Bakr (RA): The Beloved of the Beloved
meta_description: The early life of Aisha bint Abu Bakr (RA): a child born into faith, raised in the years of persecution, and married into a home that lived on little but love. The companion through whom a quarter of Islamic teaching reaches us.
keywords: Aisha bint Abu Bakr, story of Aisha, wife of Prophet Muhammad, daughter of Abu Bakr, mothers of the believers, sahaba stories, Humaira
lede: The scholars say that a quarter of our religion reaches us through one person. Not a general, not a caliph, but a young woman who grew up watching the message take root, and who later remembered every small thing about the man who carried it.

This is the story of Aisha bint Abu Bakr (may Allah be pleased with her): a child born into faith, raised in the hardest years of Makkah, and married into a home that owned almost nothing and held almost everything.

paras: When the Prophet Muhammad ﷺ left this world, Aisha was only about twenty years old. Yet from that short life lived beside him, more narrations come to us than from all of his other wives combined.

One companion, Abu Hurairah (RA), narrated a larger number of hadith overall, but most of what he reported was also reported by others. When it comes to the things only one person witnessed and could pass on, there is no companion, man or woman, who matches Aisha. Much of what we know of the Prophet's home, his prayer at night, his gentleness, comes to us through her eyes alone.

The remarkable thing is what her telling does to the reader. The person who saw him most closely praised him most warmly. Read his life through her, and you come away loving him more, and loving the one who told the story.

She was, by every account, the most beloved of his wives. If you had asked the Prophet ﷺ who was dearest to him, he would have answered her name without hesitation. And of all the homes he could have rested in, he preferred hers, as though Allah had handed her the keys to his happiness.

sections.h2: Two women, two seasons of a life
paras: It is striking how Aisha's place echoes Khadijah's. The mission of the Prophet ﷺ began in the arms of Khadijah (RA) in Makkah, and it would end, many years later, in the arms of Aisha in Madinah. Allah sent each woman at exactly the time she was needed.

Makkah had needed a Khadijah: someone to bear the weight of those first frightening years, to comfort him and steady him and spend on the cause without counting. Madinah, after the great void her death left, needed someone who could bring joy back into his home.

That was Aisha. She was full of life, and she brought life into his house and, through her countless narrations, into the pages of his story that we still read today. Far from competing with Khadijah's memory, her whole life would be marked by hearing the Prophet ﷺ mention Khadijah, a woman she had never even met.

sections.h2: The daughter of the truthful man
paras: Her father was Abdullah ibn Uthman, the man the whole world knows as Abu Bakr as-Siddiq (RA), the closest friend of the Prophet ﷺ. Her mother was Umm Ruman, a woman the Prophet ﷺ described as one of the people of Paradise.

Aisha took after her father in almost everything. He was perceptive, articulate, and honest, fond of poetry and lineage and the harder kinds of knowledge. She grew up sharp, eloquent, and quick, with that same love of poetry. And she inherited the three things that defined him most: a love of the Quran, a love of charity, and a love of the Prophet ﷺ.

The scholars gave her many names. The truthful daughter of the truthful man. The beloved one of the beloved one of Allah. The Prophet ﷺ himself called her by gentle, shortened names, and called her Humaira, the one with a reddish glow, because her face would flush when she was shy and when she was moved.

Unlike the other wives of the Prophet ﷺ, Aisha never passed through a time of disbelief. "I do not remember a time," she said, "except that my parents were Muslims." She was born into the faith and raised in it.

And she could not recall a single day of her childhood without the Prophet ﷺ visiting their house, morning and evening. So she grew up watching him, long before she would ever be his wife, and watching what belief was costing the people she loved.

She saw her father come home bruised from beatings. She remembered him stepping in with his own body when the Prophet ﷺ was attacked in public, struck once until he fell unconscious. She watched him gather the household's money and spend it freeing the persecuted, buying the freedom of Bilal and Khabbab (RA). She saw her parents weeping as they memorised the Quran, her father a man who could not control his eyes when he recited it.

sections.h2: The dream and the quiet arrangement
paras: After our mother Khadijah (RA) passed away, the Prophet ﷺ was a grieving widower and a single father, and his community could not understand why he did not remarry. Then came the dreams.

For three nights, he later told Aisha, the angel Jibril came to him carrying something wrapped in green silk. "This is your wife in this life and the next," the angel said, and inside the cloth was her image. The Prophet ﷺ told no one. "If this is from Allah," he said, "it will come to pass."

It was Khawlah bint Hakim, a relative of Khadijah and the community's matchmaker, who gently raised the subject. She suggested two women: the older, much-tried Sawda (RA), and Aisha, whom she described as the daughter of the most beloved person in the world to him. When the proposal reached Abu Bakr, his first concern was not her youth but whether it was even permitted, since he and the Prophet ﷺ were like brothers. Once that was settled, and a prior promise to another family was honourably released, the engagement was agreed, to be completed years later when she came of age.

Aisha's account of the Hijrah is told from inside Abu Bakr's household. When persecution grew unbearable, her father set out to migrate to Abyssinia, only to be turned back at the edge of the city by a non-Muslim chief who could not bear to see a man so generous and so good driven from his home.

Back in Makkah, Abu Bakr built a small place of prayer in the courtyard of his house and recited the Quran there openly. Aisha remembered the women and children of the city gathering to watch, because her father wept as he read and could not hold back his tears.

Then one day the Prophet ﷺ came at an unusual hour, his head covered, and asked to speak with Abu Bakr alone. Permission to migrate had come. When her father heard that he would travel as the Prophet's companion, he wept, and Aisha said it was the first time in her life she ever saw a man cry from sheer joy. Later, when her own camel bolted and carried her away from the caravan, she heard a voice tell her to hold the reins, and the animal turned and brought her back to safety.

In Madinah, after she came of age, the women of the Ansar prepared Aisha for her wedding, singing songs of blessing while a friend reassured her through her nervousness. This was the first wedding the people of Madinah would witness for the Prophet ﷺ, and they watched closely to learn how it should be done.

There was no feast of slaughtered animals. What they had was a single bowl of milk, sent over by a generous companion. The Prophet ﷺ drank, then handed it to Aisha. Too shy to refuse, she drank a little, then passed it to her friends at his gentle instruction.
Her dowry was fifty dirhams, the same modest amount given to all his wives. "Which of his wives," she would later ask, "was as blessed as I?"

sections.h2: A house that owned almost nothing
paras: The home Aisha entered was poor in a way that is hard to imagine. "My nephew," she once told the young man who learned much of the Prophet's life from her, "we would look at the moon, and the moon, and the moon, three new moons in two months, and no fire was lit in the house of the Prophet ﷺ." When he asked how they lived, she answered: "The two black things, dates and water."

She owned a red shirt for the house, a black cloak for going out, and one yellow dress for festivals. There was a single mat that they folded up to sleep on at night and spread out to sit on by day. When the Prophet ﷺ prayed in their tiny room and needed to prostrate, he would gently touch her leg so she could draw it back.

And in all of it, she never complained. She combed his hair, she tried to serve him, and she watched him mend his own clothes and his own sandals. She kept her childhood dolls, and once, seeing a toy horse with wings, the Prophet ﷺ asked what it was. When she reminded him that Sulaiman, peace be upon him, had horses with wings, he laughed.

Most of all, she gave. She had learned charity from her parents, and even with so little she could not stop. She would perfume the few coins she had with musk before giving them away, saying that they passed into the hand of Allah. When a mother came begging with two hungry daughters, Aisha gave away the only three dates in the house. When a gift of lamb arrived, she gave nearly all of it and kept back only the shoulder for the Prophet ﷺ, and he told her, gently, that the only part she had truly kept was what she had given away.

lessons_heading: What Aisha's early life teaches us
lessons_intro: Her story is not yet the famous scholar and teacher she would become. It is the quiet beginning: a child shaped by faith, and a young woman who found contentment in a home that had almost nothing.

lessons.bold: Children watch more than we think.
lessons.text: Aisha's deepest memories were of her father weeping over the Quran and spending his wealth to free the oppressed. The faith she inherited was the faith she had watched lived out at home.
Months would pass without a hot meal, and she never once complained. She measured the house not by what it owned but by the love and the purpose inside it.

lessons.bold: Give before you feel ready to give.
lessons.text: She had little, yet she perfumed her last coins and gave them away. Generosity was not what she did after she had enough, it was simply who she was.

lessons.bold: What you keep is what you give.
lessons.text: The lamb she gave away was the part that lasted. The story reminds us that the only things we truly hold on to are the things we let go of for the sake of Allah.

faq.q: Who was Aisha bint Abu Bakr?
faq.a: She was the daughter of Abu Bakr as-Siddiq (RA), the closest companion of the Prophet Muhammad ﷺ, and one of the Mothers of the Believers. She is remembered as a great narrator and teacher, and more reports of the Prophet's life reach us through her than through any of his other wives.

faq.q: Why is so much of Islamic knowledge linked to Aisha?
faq.a: She lived in the Prophet's home and witnessed his private life closely, then taught what she had learned for decades after him. The scholars say a quarter of the religion's teachings come through her, including many things only she could report.
It was an affectionate name from the Prophet ﷺ, meaning the one with a reddish glow. Her face would flush when she was shy and when she was moved, and the name reflected that warmth and liveliness.

faq.q: What can we learn from her early life?
faq.a: That faith is often shaped quietly at home, that contentment can outweigh hardship, and that generosity is not about having enough to spare but about a heart that gives anyway.

closing_heading: Why this story stays with us
closing: It is easy to imagine that a great life needs great means: a large house, a full table, the freedom to give without feeling it. Aisha's early years say otherwise. She lived on dates and water, slept on a folded mat, owned a single dress for festivals, and still she gave, still she found her home blessed.

Most of us will be tested the same way she was, not in front of crowds, but in our own homes and in what we do with the little we have. Her life is a quiet invitation: to watch over what our children see in us, to be content with enough, and to give before we feel we can afford to. Carry one of those into today, and her story has done its work.
source_note: The narrative follows the account of Aisha (RA) in Dr. Omar Suleiman's series The Firsts (Yaqeen Institute), covering her early years and the beginning of her life with the Prophet ﷺ. Where the historical record carries more than one narration, we have followed the most widely reported.

slides:
The scholars say a quarter of our religion reaches us through one person.

Not a general. Not a caliph.

A young woman who remembered everything.

Aisha bint Abu Bakr (RA) never knew a day of disbelief.

"I do not remember a time," she said, "except that my parents were Muslims."

Her childhood was the years of persecution.

She watched her father come home beaten. She watched him spend the family's money freeing the oppressed. She watched him weep over the Quran.

When the Prophet ﷺ heard he could finally migrate, her father wept.

Aisha said it was the first time in her life she ever saw a man cry from pure joy.

Her wedding feast was a single bowl of milk.

Her dowry was fifty dirhams.

"Which of his wives," she would ask, "was as blessed as I?"

"We would look at the moon, and the moon, and the moon, and no fire was lit in the house."

Three months at a time on dates and water. She never once complained.

She owned one dress for festivals and a single mat they folded to sleep on.

Yet she perfumed her last coins with musk before giving them away.

**What you keep is what you give.**

She gave away the lamb and kept the shoulder. He told her: the only part you truly kept is what you gave.

May Allah be pleased with her.
social.caption:
A quarter of Islamic teaching reaches us through one young woman: Aisha bint Abu Bakr (may Allah be pleased with her), the daughter of Abu Bakr as-Siddiq.

She was born into faith and raised in the hardest years of Makkah, watching her father weep over the Quran and spend his wealth freeing the oppressed. When she married the Prophet ﷺ, the wedding feast was a single bowl of milk, and the home she entered would go months without a fire lit to cook. She never once complained, and even with so little, she could not stop giving.

Which part of her story stayed with you? 🤍

Save this for later, and share it with someone who would love it.

email.subject: A quarter of our religion reaches us through her
email.alt_subject: The wedding feast was a single bowl of milk
email.preview: She was born into faith, raised in the hardest years, and married into a home that owned almost nothing.
email.body:
Assalamu alaikum,

The scholars say that a quarter of our religion reaches us through one person. Not a general, not a caliph, but a young woman: Aisha bint Abu Bakr (may Allah be pleased with her), the daughter of the Prophet's closest friend.

Unlike the other wives of the Prophet ﷺ, she never passed through a time of disbelief. "I do not remember a time," she said, "except that my parents were Muslims." She was born into the faith and raised inside its hardest years.

Her earliest memories were of the struggle in Makkah. She watched her father come home bruised from beatings. She watched him gather the household's money and spend it freeing the oppressed. And she remembered him weeping as he recited the Quran, a man who could not control his eyes when he read it.

When the Prophet ﷺ finally received permission to migrate and told her father that he would travel as his companion, Abu Bakr wept. Aisha said it was the first time in her life she ever saw a man cry from pure joy.

The home she eventually entered in Madinah was poor in a way that is hard to imagine. The wedding feast was a single bowl of milk. Her dowry was fifty dirhams. "We would look at the moon, and the moon, and the moon," she once said, "and no fire was lit in the house." Three months at a time, she and the Prophet ﷺ lived on dates and water.

She never once complained. She combed his hair, she tried to serve him, and she watched the Prophet ﷺ mend his own clothes and his own sandals. And even with so little, she could not stop giving. She would perfume her last few coins with musk before handing them away, saying that they passed into the hand of Allah.

There is a lesson in her early life that is easy to miss: contentment is its own kind of wealth, and generosity is not about having enough to spare. It is simply who you are.

May Allah be pleased with her, and fill our homes with what filled hers.

If you would like the full story, we have written it here:

paras: The scholars say that a quarter of our religion reaches us through one person. Not a general, not a caliph, but a young woman who grew up watching the message take root, and who later remembered every small thing about the man who carried it.

Aisha bint Abu Bakr (may Allah be pleased with her) was the daughter of Abu Bakr as-Siddiq, the closest friend of the Prophet ﷺ. Unlike the others, she never knew a day of disbelief. "I do not remember a time," she said, "except that my parents were Muslims."

Her childhood was the years of persecution in Makkah. She watched her father come home bruised from beatings, watched him spend the family's money freeing the oppressed, and watched him weep as he recited the Quran, unable to control his eyes when he read it.

When the Prophet ﷺ came one day at an unusual hour and told her father that they would migrate together, Abu Bakr wept. Aisha said it was the first time in her life she ever saw a man cry from pure joy.

The home she entered in Madinah owned almost nothing. The wedding feast was a single bowl of milk; her dowry was fifty dirhams. "We would look at the moon, and the moon, and the moon," she said, "and no fire was lit in the house." For months at a time they lived on dates and water, and she never once complained.

Even with so little, she could not stop giving. She perfumed her last coins with musk before handing them away. When a gift of lamb arrived, she gave nearly all of it and kept back only the shoulder, and the Prophet ﷺ told her, gently, that the only part she had truly kept was what she had given away.
storybook.pullquote: Which of his wives was as blessed as I?
storybook.reflection: Aisha's early life is not the famous scholar she would become, but the quiet beginning: a child shaped by what she saw at home, and a young woman content in a house that owned almost nothing. Her example asks something ordinary and hard of us, to be content with enough and to give before we feel we can afford to, trusting that what we let go of for Allah is the only thing we truly keep.
